sourceQuestion:
A university placement center maintains a relational database with the following schema:

COMPANY(name, location)
STUDENT(rollno, name, sdegree)
INTERVIEW(rollno, name, idate)
OFFER(rollno, name, salary)

The COMPANY relation gives the name and location of the company. The STUDENT relation gives the student's roll number, name and degree program. The INTERVIEW relation gives the date on which a student attended a company's interview. The OFFER relation gives the salary offered to a student by a company.

For the following queries, write a relational algebra expression using only the operators ><, sigma, pi, union (U), intersection (n), set difference (-), and rename (rho):

i. List the roll numbers and names of students who attended at least one interview but did not receive any job offer.

ii. List the name of the degree program in which more than five students were offered jobs from every company with which they interviewed.

iii. Write an SQL query to list, for each degree program in which students were offered jobs, the name of the degree program and the average salary of students in this degree program.

A. Names of girl students with highest marks
B. Names of girl students with marks not less than some boy student
C. Names of girl students with marks not less than any boy student
D. Names of girl students with more marks than all the boy students
sourceAnswer: C. Names of girl students with marks not less than any boy student

parsed.stem:
A university placement center maintains a relational database with the following schema:

COMPANY(name, location)
STUDENT(rollno, name, sdegree)
INTERVIEW(rollno, name, idate)
OFFER(rollno, name, salary)

The COMPANY relation gives the name and location of the company. The STUDENT relation gives the student's roll number, name and degree program. The INTERVIEW relation gives the date on which a student attended a company's interview. The OFFER relation gives the salary offered to a student by a company.

For the following queries, write a relational algebra expression using only the operators ><, sigma, pi, union (U), intersection (n), set difference (-), and rename (rho):

i. List the roll numbers and names of students who attended at least one interview but did not receive any job offer.

ii. List the name of the degree program in which more than five students were offered jobs from every company with which they interviewed.

iii. Write an SQL query to list, for each degree program in which students were offered jobs, the name of the degree program and the average salary of students in this degree program.

options.text: Names of girl students with highest marks
Names of girl students with marks not less than some boy student
Names of girl students with marks not less than any boy student
Names of girl students with more marks than all the boy students

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following SQL query:

Select distinct a1, a2, ..., an
from r1, r2, ..., rm
where P

For an arbitrary predicate P, this query is equivalent to which of the following relational algebra expressions?

A. pi_{a1,a2,...,an}(sigma_P(r1 x r2 x ... x rm))
B. pi_{a1,a2,...,an}(sigma_P(r1 |x| r2 |x| ... |x| rm))
C. sigma_P(pi_{a1,a2,...,an}(r1 x r2 x ... x rm))
D. pi_{a1,a2,...,an}(sigma_P(r1 union r2 union ... union rm))
sourceAnswer: A. pi_{a1,a2,...,an}(sigma_P(r1 x r2 x ... x rm))

parsed.stem:
Consider the following SQL query:

Select distinct a1, a2, ..., an
from r1, r2, ..., rm
where P

For an arbitrary predicate P, this query is equivalent to which of the following relational algebra expressions?

options.text: pi_{a1,a2,...,an}(sigma_P(r1 x r2 x ... x rm))
sigma_P(pi_{a1,a2,...,an}(r1 x r2 x ... x rm))
pi_{a1,a2,...,an}(sigma_P(r1 union r2 union ... union rm))

sourceQuestion:
Consider the relation Student(name, sex, marks), where the primary key is shown underlined, pertaining to students in a class that has at least one boy and one girl. What does the following relational algebra expression produce? (Note: rho is the rename operator)

rho_{temp(name', sex', marks')}(Student) ⋈_{sex='female' AND sex'='male' AND marks>=marks'} rho_{temp}(Student)

More precisely: pi_{name}(sigma_{sex='female'}(Student)) - pi_{name}(sigma_{sex='female' AND sex'='male' AND marks<marks'}(Student JOIN rho_{temp}(Student)))

A. Names of girl students with the highest marks
B. Names of girl students with more marks than some boy student
C. Names of girl students with marks not less than some boy student
D. Names of girl students with more marks than all the boy students
sourceAnswer: C. Names of girl students with marks not less than some boy student

parsed.stem:
Consider the relation Student(name, sex, marks), where the primary key is shown underlined, pertaining to students in a class that has at least one boy and one girl. What does the following relational algebra expression produce? (Note: rho is the rename operator)

rho_{temp(name', sex', marks')}(Student) ⋈_{sex='female' AND sex'='male' AND marks>=marks'} rho_{temp}(Student)

More precisely: pi_{name}(sigma_{sex='female'}(Student)) - pi_{name}(sigma_{sex='female' AND sex'='male' AND marks<marks'}(Student JOIN rho_{temp}(Student)))

options.text: Names of girl students with the highest marks
Names of girl students with more marks than some boy student
Names of girl students with marks not less than some boy student
Names of girl students with more marks than all the boy students

sourceQuestion:
Information about a collection of students is given by the relation stud(rollno, name, sex, age, marks). The relation CourseEnroll(rollno, cno) gives which student has enrolled for (or taken) what course(s). Assume that every course is taken by at least one male and at least one female student. What does the following relational algebra expression represent?

pi_{cno}(CourseEnroll) - pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex='male'}(stud) ⋈ CourseEnroll)

Wait — the actual expression from the image is:

pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)) - pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud) ⋈ sigma_{sex=male}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll))

From the image, the expression is:
T_{course}(pi_{rollno,cno}(CourseEnroll)) and T_{male}(pi_{rollno,cno}(sigma_{sex='male'}(stud) ⋈ CourseEnroll)).
The query is: pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)) - pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female AND sex'=male}(...))

Actual expression from image:
pi_{cno}((sigma_{sex=female}(stud)) ⋈ CourseEnroll) - pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud) ⋈ (sigma_{sex=male}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)))

More precisely from image: T_{male/female} notation:
pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)) - pi_{cno}((sigma_{sex=female}(stud) ⋈ CourseEnroll) ⋈_{cno=cno'} rho(sigma_{sex=male}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)))

The actual image expression: sigma_{sex=female/male} with projection of cno.

From image: pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex='female'}(stud JOIN CourseEnroll)) - pi_{cno}((sigma_{sex='female'}(stud JOIN CourseEnroll)) natural-join (pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex='male'}(stud JOIN CourseEnroll))))

A. Courses in which all female students are enrolled.
B. Courses in which a proper subset of female students are enrolled.
C. Courses in which all male students are enrolled.
D. None of the above
sourceAnswer: A. Courses in which all female students are enrolled.

parsed.stem:
Information about a collection of students is given by the relation stud(rollno, name, sex, age, marks). The relation CourseEnroll(rollno, cno) gives which student has enrolled for (or taken) what course(s). Assume that every course is taken by at least one male and at least one female student. What does the following relational algebra expression represent?

pi_{cno}(CourseEnroll) - pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex='male'}(stud) ⋈ CourseEnroll)

Wait — the actual expression from the image is:

pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)) - pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud) ⋈ sigma_{sex=male}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll))

From the image, the expression is:
T_{course}(pi_{rollno,cno}(CourseEnroll)) and T_{male}(pi_{rollno,cno}(sigma_{sex='male'}(stud) ⋈ CourseEnroll)).
The query is: pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)) - pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female AND sex'=male}(...))

Actual expression from image:
pi_{cno}((sigma_{sex=female}(stud)) ⋈ CourseEnroll) - pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud) ⋈ (sigma_{sex=male}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)))

More precisely from image: T_{male/female} notation:
pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex=female}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)) - pi_{cno}((sigma_{sex=female}(stud) ⋈ CourseEnroll) ⋈_{cno=cno'} rho(sigma_{sex=male}(stud ⋈ CourseEnroll)))

The actual image expression: sigma_{sex=female/male} with projection of cno.

From image: pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex='female'}(stud JOIN CourseEnroll)) - pi_{cno}((sigma_{sex='female'}(stud JOIN CourseEnroll)) natural-join (pi_{cno}(sigma_{sex='male'}(stud JOIN CourseEnroll))))

options.text: Courses in which all female students are enrolled.
Courses in which a proper subset of female students are enrolled.
Courses in which all male students are enrolled.

sourceQuestion:
Let R and S be two relations with the following schema where {P, Q} is the key for both schemas.
R(P, Q, R1, R2, R3)
S(P, Q, S1, S2)

Which of the following queries are equivalent?
I.   pi_{P,Q}(R ⋈ S)
II.  pi_{P}(R ⋈ S) ⋈ pi_{Q}(R ⋈ S)
III. pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(S)
IV.  pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(R) (Note: this should be III as written — from image: IV is pi_{P,Q}((pi_{P,Q}(R)) ⋈ (pi_{P,Q}(S))))

Actual from image:
I.   pi_{P,Q}(R ⋈ S)
II.  pi_{P}(R ⋈ S) ⋈ pi_{Q}(R ⋈ S)
III. pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(S)
IV.  pi_{P,Q}(pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(S))

A. Only I and II
B. Only I and III
C. Only I, II and III
D. Only I, III and IV
sourceAnswer: D. Only I, III and IV

parsed.stem:
Let R and S be two relations with the following schema where {P, Q} is the key for both schemas.
R(P, Q, R1, R2, R3)
S(P, Q, S1, S2)

Which of the following queries are equivalent?
I.   pi_{P,Q}(R ⋈ S)
II.  pi_{P}(R ⋈ S) ⋈ pi_{Q}(R ⋈ S)
III. pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(S)
IV.  pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(R) (Note: this should be III as written — from image: IV is pi_{P,Q}((pi_{P,Q}(R)) ⋈ (pi_{P,Q}(S))))

Actual from image:
I.   pi_{P,Q}(R ⋈ S)
II.  pi_{P}(R ⋈ S) ⋈ pi_{Q}(R ⋈ S)
III. pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(S)
IV.  pi_{P,Q}(pi_{P,Q}(R) ⋈ pi_{P,Q}(S))

sourceQuestion:
What is the optimized version of the relational algebra expression sigma_{P1}(sigma_{P2}(Pi_{A1,A2}(r))), where A1, A2 are sets of attributes in r with A1 subset A2 and P1, P2 are Boolean expressions based on the attributes in A1?

A. Pi_{A1}(sigma_{P1 AND P2}(r))
B. sigma_{P1 AND P2}(Pi_{A1}(r))
C. Pi_{A1}(sigma_{P1 AND P2}(Pi_{A2}(r)))
D. Only I, III and IV

parsed.stem: What is the optimized version of the relational algebra expression sigma_{P1}(sigma_{P2}(Pi_{A1,A2}(r))), where A1, A2 are sets of attributes in r with A1 subset A2 and P1, P2 are Boolean expressions based on the attributes in A1?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the relational schema given below, where eId of the relation dependent is a foreign key referring to empId of the relation employee. Assume that every employee has at least one associated dependent in the dependent relation.

employee (empId, empName, empAge)
dependent (depId, eId, depName, depAge)

The above query evaluates to the set of empIds of employees whose age is greater than that of all of his/her dependents.

Pi_{empId}(employee) - Pi_{empId}(sigma_{empAge <= depAge}(employee bowtie_{empId=eId} dependent))

A. some dependent.
B. all dependents.
C. some of his/her dependents.
D. all of his/her dependents.

parsed.stem:
Consider the relational schema given below, where eId of the relation dependent is a foreign key referring to empId of the relation employee. Assume that every employee has at least one associated dependent in the dependent relation.

employee (empId, empName, empAge)
dependent (depId, eId, depName, depAge)

The above query evaluates to the set of empIds of employees whose age is greater than that of all of his/her dependents.

Pi_{empId}(employee) - Pi_{empId}(sigma_{empAge <= depAge}(employee bowtie_{empId=eId} dependent))

sourceQuestion:
SELECT operation in SQL is equivalent to

A. The selection operation in relational algebra
B. The selection operation in relational algebra, except that SELECT in SQL retains duplicates
C. The projection operation in relational algebra
D. The projection operation in relational algebra, except that SELECT in SQL retains duplicates
sourceAnswer: D. The projection operation in relational algebra, except that SELECT in SQL retains duplicates

parsed.stem: SELECT operation in SQL is equivalent to

options.text: The selection operation in relational algebra
The selection operation in relational algebra, except that SELECT in SQL retains duplicates
The projection operation in relational algebra
The projection operation in relational algebra, except that SELECT in SQL retains duplicates

sourceQuestion:
Consider the relations r(A, B) and s(B, C), where B is a primary key in s and r.B is a foreign key referencing s. Consider the query Q : r join (sigma_{B=5}(s)). Let LOJ denote the natural left outer-join operation. Assume that r and s contain no null values. Which of the following is NOT equivalent to Q?
A. sigma_{B=5}(r join s)
B. sigma_{B=5}(r LOJ s)
C. r LOJ (sigma_{B=5}(s))
D. sigma_{B=5}(r) join s

parsed.stem: Consider the relations r(A, B) and s(B, C), where B is a primary key in s and r.B is a foreign key referencing s. Consider the query Q : r join (sigma_{B=5}(s)). Let LOJ denote the natural left outer-join operation. Assume that r and s contain no null values. Which of the following is NOT equivalent to Q?

sourceQuestion:
The following relation records the age of 500 employees of a company, where empNo (indicating the employee number) is the key:

emp(empNo, age)

Consider the following relational algebra expression:

Pi_{empNo}(emp) - Pi_{empNo}(sigma_{age < age2}(rho_{empNo2,age2}(emp) x emp))

What does the above expression generate?

A. Employee numbers of only those employees whose age is the maximum
B. Employee numbers of only those employees whose age is more than the age of exactly one other employee
C. Employee numbers of those employees whose age is not the minimum
D. Employee numbers of all employees whose age is the minimum
sourceAnswer: A. Employee numbers of only those employees whose age is the maximum

parsed.stem:
The following relation records the age of 500 employees of a company, where empNo (indicating the employee number) is the key:

emp(empNo, age)

Consider the following relational algebra expression:

Pi_{empNo}(emp) - Pi_{empNo}(sigma_{age < age2}(rho_{empNo2,age2}(emp) x emp))

What does the above expression generate?

options.text: Employee numbers of only those employees whose age is the maximum
Employee numbers of only those employees whose age is more than the age of exactly one other employee
Employee numbers of those employees whose age is not the minimum
Employee numbers of all employees whose age is the minimum

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following three relations in a relational database:

Employee(id, Name)
Brand(brandName)
Owns(id, brandName)

Which of the following relational algebra expressions return the set of id's who own all the brands?

A. Pi_{id}(Owns) / Pi_{brandName}(Brand)
B. Pi_{id}(Owns) / Pi_{brandName}(Owns)
C. Pi_{id}(Owns) / (Pi_{brandName}(Brand) - Pi_{brandName}(Owns))
D. Pi_{id,brandName}(Owns) / Pi_{brandName}(Brand)

parsed.stem:
Consider the following three relations in a relational database:

Employee(id, Name)
Brand(brandName)
Owns(id, brandName)

Which of the following relational algebra expressions return the set of id's who own all the brands?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following two relations, R(A, B) and S(A, C):

R:
A  | B
---+----
10 | 20
20 | 30
30 | 40
40 | 30
50 | 95

S:
A  | C
---+----
10 | 20
20 | 10
30 | 45
40 | 80

The total number of tuples obtained by evaluating the following expression:

sigma_{B > A}(R >< S)

is ___

A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4

---+----

50 | 95

S:
A  | C
---+----
10 | 20
20 | 10
30 | 45
40 | 80

The total number of tuples obtained by evaluating the following expression:

sigma_{B > A}(R >< S)

is ___

sourceQuestion:
The relation schema, Person (pid, city), describes the city of residence for every person uniquely identified by pid. The following relational algebra operators are available: selection, projection, cross product, and rename.

To find the list of cities where at least 3 persons reside, using the above operators, the minimum number of cross product operations that must be used is:

A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4

parsed.stem:
The relation schema, Person (pid, city), describes the city of residence for every person uniquely identified by pid. The following relational algebra operators are available: selection, projection, cross product, and rename.

To find the list of cities where at least 3 persons reside, using the above operators, the minimum number of cross product operations that must be used is:

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following three relations:
Car (model, year, serial, color)
Make (maker, model)
Own (owner, serial)
A tuple in Car represents a specific car of a given model, made in a given year, with a serial number and a color. A tuple in Make specifies that a company makes cars of a certain model. A tuple in Own specifies that an owner owns the car with a given serial number. Keys are underlined; (owner, serial) together form the key for Own (serial denotes natural join).

pi_owner(Own |><| (sigma_{color='red'}(Car) |><| sigma_{maker='ABC'}(Make)))

Which one of the following options describes what the above expression computes?
A. All owners of a red car, a car made by ABC, or a red car made by ABC
B. All owners of more than one car, where at least one car is red and made by ABC
C. All owners of a car made by ABC
D. All owners of all red cars made by ABC
sourceAnswer: D. All owners of all red cars made by ABC

parsed.stem:
Consider the following three relations:
Car (model, year, serial, color)
Make (maker, model)
Own (owner, serial)
A tuple in Car represents a specific car of a given model, made in a given year, with a serial number and a color. A tuple in Make specifies that a company makes cars of a certain model. A tuple in Own specifies that an owner owns the car with a given serial number. Keys are underlined; (owner, serial) together form the key for Own (serial denotes natural join).

pi_owner(Own |><| (sigma_{color='red'}(Car) |><| sigma_{maker='ABC'}(Make)))

Which one of the following options describes what the above expression computes?

options.text: All owners of a red car, a car made by ABC, or a red car made by ABC
All owners of more than one car, where at least one car is red and made by ABC
All owners of a car made by ABC
All owners of all red cars made by ABC

sourceQuestion:
Consider a database that includes the following relations:
Defender(name, rating, side, goals)
Forward(name, rating, assists, goals)
Team(name, club, price)

Which ONE of the following relational algebra expressions checks that every name occurring in Team appears in either Defender or Forward, where phi denotes the empty set?

A. (pi_name(Team) - (pi_name(Defender) union pi_name(Forward))) = phi
B. (pi_name(Defender) union pi_name(Forward)) - pi_name(Team) = phi
C. (pi_name(Defender) intersect pi_name(Forward)) - pi_name(Team) = phi
D. (pi_name(Defender) union pi_name(Forward)) intersect pi_name(Team) = phi

parsed.stem:
Consider a database that includes the following relations:
Defender(name, rating, side, goals)
Forward(name, rating, assists, goals)
Team(name, club, price)

Which ONE of the following relational algebra expressions checks that every name occurring in Team appears in either Defender or Forward, where phi denotes the empty set?

sourceQuestion:
A table 'student' with schema (roll, name, hostel, marks), and another table 'hobby' with schema (roll, hobbyname) contains records as shown below:

student table:
Roll | Name           | Hostel | Marks
1798 | Manoj Rathor   | 7      | 95
2154 | Soumic Banerjee | 7     | 67
2369 | Gaurav Reddy   | 7      | 76
2581 | Pradeep penube | 6      | 92
2643 | Sihan Kulkarni | 5      | 78
2711 | Nitin Kodimur  | 6      | 83
2872 | Kiran Vora     | 5      | 92
3026 | Manoj Kulkarni | 6      | 94
3125 | Hemant Karkhaanis | 7   | 88
3125 | Rajesh Doshi   | 5      | 82

hobby table:
Roll | Hobby Name
1798 | chess
1798 | music
2154 | music
2643 | cricket
2643 | hockey
2711 | volleyball
2872 | cricket
3125 | photography
3125 | chess

The following SQL query is executed on the above tables:
select hostel
from student join hobby
where marks > 75 and roll between 2000 and 3000

Relations S and H with the same schema as those of these two tables respectively contain the same information as tuples. A new relation S' is obtained by the following relational algebra operation:
S' = pi_{hostel}(sigma_{marks>75 and 2000<=roll<=3000}(S) |><| H)

The number of rows output by S' is:
A. 6
B. 4
C. 2
D. 0

3125 | chess

The number of rows output by S' is:

sourceQuestion:
The following relations are used to store data about students, courses, enrollment of students in courses and teachers of courses. Attributes for primary key in each relation are marked by '*':

students(rollno*, sname, saddr)
courses(cno*, cname)
enroll(rollno*, cno*, grade)
teach(tno*, tname, cno)

(cno is course number, cname is course name, tno is teacher number, tname is teacher name, sname is student name, etc.)

For the relational database given above, the following functional dependencies hold:
- rollno -> sname, saddr
- cno -> cname
- tno -> tname
- rollno, cno -> grade

a. Is the database in 3NF?
b. If yes, prove that it is in 3NF (without proving).

Which of the following relational tuple calculus expressions is equivalent to the following tuple calculus expression:
{ t | t in R_1(A_1) AND p_1(R_1) -> p_2(R_2) }

Where C1 and C2 are selection conditions and A1 and A2 are attributes of R1.

A. sigma_{C1}(R_1) union sigma_{C2}(R_1)
B. Pi_{A1}(R_1) |x| Pi_{A2}(R_2)
C. sigma_{C1}(R_1) -> sigma_{C2}(R_1)
D. sigma_{C1 AND C2}(R_1)

parsed.stem:
The following relations are used to store data about students, courses, enrollment of students in courses and teachers of courses. Attributes for primary key in each relation are marked by '*':

students(rollno*, sname, saddr)
courses(cno*, cname)
enroll(rollno*, cno*, grade)
teach(tno*, tname, cno)

(cno is course number, cname is course name, tno is teacher number, tname is teacher name, sname is student name, etc.)

For the relational database given above, the following functional dependencies hold:
- rollno -> sname, saddr
- cno -> cname
- tno -> tname
- rollno, cno -> grade

a. Is the database in 3NF?
b. If yes, prove that it is in 3NF (without proving).

Which of the following relational tuple calculus expressions is equivalent to the following tuple calculus expression:
{ t | t in R_1(A_1) AND p_1(R_1) -> p_2(R_2) }

Where C1 and C2 are selection conditions and A1 and A2 are attributes of R1.

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following query transformations (i.e., replacing the l.h.s. expression by the r.h.s. expression) is incorrect? R1 and R2 are relations, C1 and C2 are selection conditions and A1 and A2 are attributes of R1.

A. sigma_{C1}(R1 bowtie R2) -> sigma_{C1}(R1) bowtie R2
B. pi_{A1}(R1 bowtie R2) -> pi_{A1}(sigma_{A2}(R1)) bowtie R2
C. sigma_{C1}(R1 bowtie R2) -> sigma_{C1}(R1) bowtie sigma_{C2}(R2)
D. sigma_{C1}(R1 bowtie R2) -> sigma_{C1}(R1) bowtie R2
sourceAnswer: B. pi_{A1}(R1 bowtie R2) -> pi_{A1}(sigma_{A2}(R1)) bowtie R2

parsed.stem: Which of the following query transformations (i.e., replacing the l.h.s. expression by the r.h.s. expression) is incorrect? R1 and R2 are relations, C1 and C2 are selection conditions and A1 and A2 are attributes of R1.

options.text: sigma_{C1}(R1 bowtie R2) -> sigma_{C1}(R1) bowtie R2
pi_{A1}(R1 bowtie R2) -> pi_{A1}(sigma_{A2}(R1)) bowtie R2
sigma_{C1}(R1 bowtie R2) -> sigma_{C1}(R1) bowtie sigma_{C2}(R2)
sigma_{C1}(R1 bowtie R2) -> sigma_{C1}(R1) bowtie R2

sourceQuestion:
The relational algebra expression equivalent to the following tuple calculus expression is:

{t | t in r ^ (t[A] = 10 ^ t[B] = 20)}

A. sigma_{A=10}(r) ^ sigma_{B=20}(r)
B. sigma_{A=10, B=20}(r)
C. pi_{A=10, B=20}(r)
D. sigma_{A=10}(r) bowtie sigma_{B=20}(r)

parsed.stem:
The relational algebra expression equivalent to the following tuple calculus expression is:

{t | t in r ^ (t[A] = 10 ^ t[B] = 20)}

sourceAnswer: B. {t | forall u in R1(t[A] = u[A])}

parsed.stem: Which of the following relational calculus expressions is not safe?

options.text: {t | exists u in R1(t[A] = u[A]) ^ -exists u in R2(t[A] = u[A])}
{t | exists u in R1(t[A] = u[A]) ^ t[A] = 5}

sourceQuestion:
With regards to the expressive power of the formal relational query languages, which of the following statements is true?
A. Relational algebra is more powerful than relational calculus
B. Relational algebra has the same power as relational calculus
C. Relational algebra has the same power as safe relational calculus
D. None of the above
sourceAnswer: C. Relational algebra has the same power as safe relational calculus

parsed.stem: With regards to the expressive power of the formal relational query languages, which of the following statements is true?

options.text: Relational algebra is more powerful than relational calculus
Relational algebra has the same power as relational calculus
Relational algebra has the same power as safe relational calculus

sourceQuestion:
Let R_1(A, B, C) and R_2(D, B, E) be two relation schemas, where the primary keys are shown underlined, and let C be a foreign key in R_1 referring to R_2. Suppose there is no violation of the above referential integrity constraint in the corresponding relation instances r_1 and r_2. Which of the following relational algebra expressions would necessarily produce an empty relation?
A. Pi_{B}(r_1) - Pi_{B}(r_2)
B. Pi_{A}(r_1 bowtie_{r_1.C = r_2.D} r_2)
C. Pi_{B}(r_1 bowtie_{r_1.B = r_2.B} r_2)
D. Pi_{C}(r_1) - Pi_{D}(r_2)

parsed.stem: Let R_1(A, B, C) and R_2(D, B, E) be two relation schemas, where the primary keys are shown underlined, and let C be a foreign key in R_1 referring to R_2. Suppose there is no violation of the above referential integrity constraint in the corresponding relation instances r_1 and r_2. Which of the following relational algebra expressions would necessarily produce an empty relation?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the relation employee(name, sex, supervisorName) with name as the key, supervisorName gives the name of the supervisor of the employee under consideration. What does the following Tuple Relational Calculus query produce?
{ e.name | employee(e) AND (there exists x)(employee(x) AND x.supervisorName = e.name AND x.sex = 'male') }
A. Names of employees with a male supervisor.
B. Names of employees who supervise a male employee.
C. Names of employees with no immediate female subordinates.
D. Names of employees who supervise a female employee.
sourceAnswer: B. Names of employees who supervise a male employee.

parsed.stem:
Consider the relation employee(name, sex, supervisorName) with name as the key, supervisorName gives the name of the supervisor of the employee under consideration. What does the following Tuple Relational Calculus query produce?
{ e.name | employee(e) AND (there exists x)(employee(x) AND x.supervisorName = e.name AND x.sex = 'male') }

options.text: Names of employees with a male supervisor.
Names of employees who supervise a male employee.
Names of employees with no immediate female subordinates.
Names of employees who supervise a female employee.

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following tuple relational calculus expression(s) is/are equivalent to for_all t in r(P(t)) (universal quantification, i.e., P holds for all tuples t in r)?
I.   NOT (exists t)(r(t) AND NOT P(t))
II.  NOT (exists t)(NOT r(t) AND P(t))
III. (exists t)(r(t) AND NOT P(t)) is FALSE
IV.  NOT (exists t)(r(t) AND NOT P(t))

A. I only
B. II only
C. III only
D. III and IV only

parsed.stem:
Which of the following tuple relational calculus expression(s) is/are equivalent to for_all t in r(P(t)) (universal quantification, i.e., P holds for all tuples t in r)?
I.   NOT (exists t)(r(t) AND NOT P(t))
II.  NOT (exists t)(NOT r(t) AND P(t))
III. (exists t)(r(t) AND NOT P(t)) is FALSE
IV.  NOT (exists t)(r(t) AND NOT P(t))

sourceQuestion:
Let R and S be relational schemes such that R = {a, b, c} and S = {c}. Now consider the following queries on the database:
1. Pi_{R-S}(R) - Pi_{R-S}(Pi_{R-S}(R) x S - R)
2. { t | t in Pi_{R-S}(R) AND NOT (exists u)(u in S AND NOT (tu in R)) }
Which of the above queries are equivalent?
A. 1 and 2
B. 1 only
C. 2 only
D. 2 and 3 and 4

parsed.stem:
Let R and S be relational schemes such that R = {a, b, c} and S = {c}. Now consider the following queries on the database:
1. Pi_{R-S}(R) - Pi_{R-S}(Pi_{R-S}(R) x S - R)
2. { t | t in Pi_{R-S}(R) AND NOT (exists u)(u in S AND NOT (tu in R)) }
Which of the above queries are equivalent?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following relational schema:
Students(rollno: integer, sname: string)
Courses(courseno: integer, cname: string)
Registration(rollno: integer, courseno: integer, percent: real)

Which of the following queries are equivalent to this query in English?
"Find the distinct names of all students who score more than 90% in the course numbered 107"

I. SELECT DISTINCT S.sname FROM Students as S, Registration as R WHERE S.rollno = R.rollno AND R.courseno = 107 AND R.percent > 90

II. {t[sname] | (Exists S in Students)(Exists R in Registration)(S.sname = t[sname] AND S.rollno = R.rollno AND R.courseno = 107 AND R.percent > 90)}

III. {S | S in Students AND (Exists R in Registration)(S.rollno = R.rollno AND R.courseno = 107 AND R.percent > 90)}

IV. {(S1, S2, S3) | (Exists R1, R2, R3)((S1, S2, S3) in Students AND (R1, R2, R3) in Registration AND S1 = R1 AND R2 = 107 AND R3 > 90)}

A. 1 and 2  B. 1 and 3  C. 2 and 4  D. 3 and 4

Which of the following relational query languages have the same expressive power?
I. Relational algebra
II. Tuple relational calculus restricted to safe expressions
III. Domain relational calculus restricted to safe expressions

A. II and III only  B. I and II only  C. I and III only  D. I, II and III

parsed.stem:
Which of the following relational query languages have the same expressive power?
I. Relational algebra
II. Tuple relational calculus restricted to safe expressions
III. Domain relational calculus restricted to safe expressions

sourceQuestion:
Consider a selection of the form sigma_{A <= v <= B}(r), where r is a relation with 1000 tuples. Assume that the attribute values for A among the tuples are uniformly distributed in the interval [0, 500]. Which one of the following options is the best estimate of the number of tuples returned by the given selection query (where v = pi approximately 3.1416 and B = 100)?

A. 50
B. 190
C. 150
D. 200

parsed.stem: Consider a selection of the form sigma_{A <= v <= B}(r), where r is a relation with 1000 tuples. Assume that the attribute values for A among the tuples are uniformly distributed in the interval [0, 500]. Which one of the following options is the best estimate of the number of tuples returned by the given selection query (where v = pi approximately 3.1416 and B = 100)?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following relational schema:
  Student(school-id, sch-roll-no, sname, saddress)
  Enrolment(school-id, sch-roll-no, exam-name, emp-place)
  ExamResult(eno, exam-name, examiner, marks)

Consider the following tuple relational calculus query:
{ t | exists E in Enrolment ( t[school-id] = E[school-id] and
    forall x in Student ( x[school-id] = t[school-id] ->
      exists e in Enrolment ( e[school-id] = x[school-id] and e[sch-roll-no] = x[sch-roll-no] and
        forall r in ExamResult ( r[exam-name] = e[exam-name] -> r[marks] > 35 ) ) ) ) }

If a student needs to score more than 35 marks to pass an exam, what does the query return?

A. The empty set
B. Schools with more than 50% of its students enrolled in some exam or the other
C. Schools with at least one student who has passed in every exam that the student has appeared for
D. Schools where every student has passed in every exam that the student has appeared for
sourceAnswer: D. Schools where every student has passed in every exam that the student has appeared for
